codeReview.summary: Solid implementation of Chef Kyla's Story page with all 7 required sections. The code follows project conventions closely: zero border-radius, no box-shadows, proper Tailwind v4 design tokens (bg-cream, text-primary, etc.), serif/sans typography system, section-padding + max-container layout, Next.js Image with data-placeholder, and semantic heading hierarchy. Server component by default (no 'use client'), SEO metadata via Next.js Metadata API, Button as='a' for CTA link. Philosophy section smartly reuses PHILOSOPHY_PILLARS from constants.ts. Credentials use an icon-based 2x2 grid with inline SVGs. All content accurately reflects Chef Kyla's story per the feature spec — obesity, hypertension, cholesterol, cortisol healing journey. Community impact covers food deserts, Watts farms, and Compton wellness center as specified.

issues.description: TransformationStory section uses a single centered column layout (max-w-3xl mx-auto) rather than a two-column layout. The task-level description mentions 'Two-column transformation narrative section', though the actual features.json spec does not specify two-column. Current single-column layout is a reasonable editorial design choice for long-form narrative, but worth noting the discrepancy with the higher-level brief.

CTA Button uses as='a' which renders a plain <a> tag for the /meal-plans internal link. This causes full-page navigation instead of client-side SPA navigation. While this follows the AGENTS.md convention ('Prefer <Button as="a" href="/path">'), it's a known trade-off in the project's pattern — every CTA link causes a full reload. Not a bug per project conventions, but noted for awareness.

Credentials data (titles, descriptions, SVG icons) is hardcoded inline in the component rather than being extracted to src/lib/constants.ts. AGENTS.md states 'All content in src/lib/constants.ts with clear sections'. Community impact initiatives data (line 290) is similarly inline. Philosophy pillars correctly use constants. Not blocking since the data is page-specific and the component is clean, but deviates slightly from the documented data architecture convention.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker inlined content data (credentials array, community initiatives array) directly in the component instead of extracting to src/lib/constants.ts. The AGENTS.md convention says 'All content in src/lib/constants.ts with clear sections'. Other pages (homepage) put all content in constants.ts. This is a minor inconsistency but may lead to mixed patterns if not clarified. AGENTS.md could note whether page-specific static data arrays that aren't shared should also go in constants.ts or can live in the component.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: src/components/about/AboutContent.tsx lines 106-222 (credentials array) and lines 290-320 (initiatives array) are defined inline, while PHILOSOPHY_PILLARS is imported from constants.ts

sharedStateObservations.observation: The feature's higher-level description mentioned 'two-column transformation narrative' but the features.json spec the worker received said 'Transformation story section - high-level narrative' without layout specifics. The worker implemented a single-column centered layout. This gap between the high-level brief and the features.json spec could cause discrepancies if the brief intent was a specific visual layout.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Task description mentions 'Two-column transformation narrative section with text about her personal health journey' vs features.json description: 'Transformation story section - high-level narrative about how she healed herself'

summary: About page passes review. All 7 required sections are implemented correctly with proper content, accessibility, and adherence to the Soul Good design system. The code is clean, well-organized, and follows project conventions (zero border-radius, Tailwind v4 tokens, Next.js Image, server component, SEO metadata). Three non-blocking issues noted: (1) transformation story uses single-column instead of two-column layout per the higher-level brief, (2) CTA uses plain <a> rather than Next.js Link (but this follows project convention), (3) some content data is inlined rather than extracted to constants.ts. None of these are blocking — the page functions correctly and delivers the required content and user experience.
